description: a1-01 demands verification of input identity between C++ and Rust sides. a2-18 describes golden data as binary snapshots from gen_golden.cpp without mentioning input provenance metadata, meaning the harness architect's oracle design lacks the input identity verification the purist requires.

a1-01 requires verifying that C++ and Rust receive the same inputs. a3-01 reveals the C++ generator uses StubClipboard/StubScreen/headless scheduler rather than production Eagle Mode, meaning even if input identity is verified, it is identity with a bespoke test harness, not with production behavior.

a1-02 demands exhaustive comparison of every output channel C++ produces. a2-17 enumerates 8 output domains with varying tolerance regimes. The tension is whether the 8 categories fully cover all C++ output channels or if channels exist that fall outside these categories, potentially violating a1-02's exhaustiveness requirement.

a1-02 requires every output channel to have a golden comparison. a3-03 identifies that the alpha channel is permanently excluded from image comparison, directly violating the exhaustive output comparison requirement.

a1-02 demands exhaustive output comparison. a3-14 shows only ~15% of Rust source files are exercised by golden tests, meaning exhaustive comparison applies only to a small fraction of the codebase, severely undermining the requirement's intent.

a1-03 requires the harness to support four-category classification of disagreements (port fault, oracle fault, specification ambiguity, acceptable difference). a2-19's 7-phase pipeline has a classification phase but only categorizes pass/fail/suspicious/regression, not the root-cause taxonomy a1-03 demands.

a1-03 requires thorough investigation of each disagreement with root-cause classification. a3-13 identifies that LLMs are structurally incentivized to widen tolerances rather than investigate disagreements, meaning the investigation process a1-03 demands is undermined by the primary development tool's incentive structure.

a1-04 and a3-09 both identify the failure independence violation, but frame it differently: a1-04 calls it 'the worst case for Vouk's detection probability model' while a3-09 concludes that golden tests 'verify rather than detect' misunderstandings. These compatible framings create a compound pessimism that could justify nihilistic conclusions about the entire testing approach.

a1-05 argues generated test inputs are essential to differential testing. a2-18 describes the oracle as binary snapshots from a pre-executed C++ generator. Generated inputs require re-executing the C++ oracle for each new input, which the frozen oracle model cannot support, making these two approaches mutually exclusive without major architectural changes.

a1-05 asserts generated inputs are essential. a3-10 identifies the current harness uses only fixed handcrafted inputs. Both agree on the gap, but a3-10 implicitly acknowledges the practical difficulty (generating valid UI tree configurations is non-trivial), while a1-05 treats it as a requirement without fully addressing feasibility.

a1-06 forbids undocumented #[ignore] annotations. a2-13 enforces this via grep-based self-conformance checks. These are largely aligned, but a2-13's syntactic grep could miss semantic equivalents (#[cfg(not(...))]) that a1-06 would still consider violations, creating a coverage gap in enforcement.

a1-06 forbids suppressing failures without documentation. a3-04 reveals that require_golden!() silently reports success when golden data is absent, achieving the same suppression effect as #[ignore] but through a different mechanism that a1-06's prohibition does not cover.

Internal a1 tension surfaced for cross-agent relevance: a1-07 says sub-tolerance treatment is a design choice not addressed by sources, while a1-13 demands MEASURE_DIVERGENCE be always-on to report all sub-tolerance disagreements. This internal inconsistency weakens a1's position when a2 or a3 propositions interact with either claim.

a1-10 argues that 'blessing' Rust output as golden data provides zero fault detection power and is methodologically dangerous. a2-16 provides a structured mechanism for exactly this: marking failures as intentional divergence with diverged=true, effectively formalizing the blessing process a1-10 condemns.

a1-10 insists golden data must come from C++ execution, not Rust. a3-17 notes the C++ reference is frozen at 0.96.4 and may encode bugs. Together they create a dilemma: the oracle is both sacrosanct (a1-10) and potentially buggy (a3-17), with no clear resolution when a C++ bug is discovered.

a1-11 requires recording compiler versions and source revisions in golden data. a3-02 notes the golden binary format lacks even basic safeguards like magic numbers or checksums. These are complementary gaps but create tension: a1-11's metadata requirements are moot if the binary format itself (a3-02) cannot reliably carry or validate that metadata.

a1-12 requires quantitative disagreement metrics (pixel percentages, max channel diff, distributions). a3-15 notes MEASURE_DIVERGENCE exists but is opt-in, outputs only to stderr, and has no storage or comparison mechanism, meaning the quantitative metrics a1-12 demands exist in primitive form but lack the infrastructure to fulfill the requirement.

a1-13 demands always-on divergence reporting for all sub-tolerance disagreements. a2-05 defines 'suspicious' only when failure percentage exceeds 50% of threshold or max_diff exceeds 75% of tolerance. The architect's suspicious threshold creates a gap below which sub-tolerance divergence goes unreported, contradicting the purist's always-on requirement.

a1-14 demands full automation of the generate-execute-compare cycle without human intervention. a2-01 proposes shell scripts and Unix pipes as the harness, which can automate the Rust side but cannot automate the C++ generation step that requires a separate build environment, making full automation impractical in the proposed architecture.

a1-14 requires automating the full generate-execute-compare cycle per McKeeman. a3-21 identifies that the frozen oracle converts the relational property into a weaker unary property. Even if full automation were achieved, the frozen oracle fundamentally cannot support the live differential comparison that McKeeman's automated cycle requires.

a1-15 requires golden tests to test observable behavior, not implementation artifacts. a3-11 identifies that settle() uses a fixed iteration count (5), which is an implementation artifact of temporal discretization rather than observable behavior. Tests depending on exactly 5 iterations could break under refactoring, violating a1-15.

a1-16 notes the asymmetric oracle relationship (C++ as authority) is a project choice, not methodological. a2-16 provides the diverged=true mechanism for when Rust intentionally departs from C++, implicitly acknowledging the asymmetry is sometimes wrong. Together they undermine the simple oracle model without providing a clear replacement.

a1-17 requires detecting golden data staleness against the current C++ source. a2-02's contract registry enumerates test cases and tolerances but does not include C++ source revision metadata per test, meaning the contract cannot support the staleness detection a1-17 requires without schema extension.

a1-18 requires that tolerances be tight enough that only the correct implementation passes. a3-07 documents that tolerances have ratcheted monotonically looser over time (up to ch_tol=3, max_fail=10%), with no tracking or prevention mechanism, directly undermining the tight-tolerance requirement.

a1-18 requires tests specific enough that only the correct implementation satisfies them. a3-08 identifies that the comparison function has no cap on how wrong individual failing pixels can be, meaning a completely wrong scanline can pass the test if few enough pixels are affected, allowing wrong implementations to satisfy the test.

a1-19 requires documenting methodology deviations at the exact point of divergence. a3-12 identifies that LLM sessions lack provenance on why previous tolerances were set, meaning documentation written in one session may not survive or be accessible in subsequent sessions, undermining the documentation requirement's effectiveness.

a1-20 requires the harness to identify which code paths have golden coverage and report gaps. a3-14 reveals the actual gap is enormous (~85% of files uncovered). If the harness reports this gap, the sheer size of it may make the requirement feel unactionable, creating organizational tension between knowing the gap and being able to address it.

a1-21 requires distinguishing regressions from pre-existing failures. a2-08 implements this via a 'latest' symlink comparing consecutive runs. The tension is that the symlink only compares against the immediately previous run, so a pre-existing failure that was temporarily fixed and then regressed looks like a new regression rather than a recurrence.

a1-23 insists every gap introduced by the frozen oracle must be justified as a deviation from published methodology. a2-18 presents the frozen oracle model without acknowledging it as a methodological deviation, treating it as the natural architecture rather than a compromise requiring documentation.

a1-25 warns that losing the C++ build environment permanently freezes the test suite. a2-22 hardcodes 214 golden data files as the registry count, implicitly treating the current set as complete rather than extensible, which accelerates the problem a1-25 identifies by treating the frozen state as the target state.

a2-02 proposes a contract registry as the single source of truth for all golden tests. a3-04 reveals require_golden!() silently passes when data is absent. The contract registry cannot serve as truth if the test execution layer silently skips entries, as the registry would show 214 tests while the actual execution silently runs zero.

a2-03 requires the harness to refuse to run if contract entries lack golden files. a3-04 shows the current macro silently succeeds when golden data is absent. These are not directly exclusive, but implementing a2-03 would change the behavior a3-04 describes, meaning one of these states must not persist.

a2-04 proposes that tolerance changes can only tighten (loosening requires audited justification). a3-07 documents that tolerances have already ratcheted to loose levels. The tension is whether a2-04's mechanism would be retroactive (requiring justification for all existing loose tolerances) or only forward-looking (locking in the already-loose status quo).

a2-04 requires a separate script with --reason for tolerance loosening. a3-13 identifies LLMs as structurally incentivized to widen tolerances. The audit mechanism in a2-04 is designed for human accountability, but an LLM can trivially provide a plausible --reason string without genuine investigation, weakening the guardrail.

a2-05 defines 'suspicious' tests based on proximity to failure thresholds (50% of max_failure_pct, 75% of channel_tolerance). a3-08 identifies that the comparison lacks a magnitude cap on failing pixels. A test with a few pixels at diff=255 would not trigger suspicious status under a2-05's criteria because the pixel count is low, even though the error is catastrophic.

a2-09 defines convergence as zero failures/regressions with non-increasing suspicious count across 3 runs. a3-25 argues the coverage gap can widen even as tests are added because the state space grows combinatorially. Convergence in a2-09's terms (stable pass rate) does not imply convergence in coverage, meaning the harness can declare convergence while coverage deteriorates.

a2-10 verifies test independence by comparing sequential and parallel execution results. a3-18 notes parallel rendering tests prove determinism but not correctness. Together they suggest the independence verification effort (which doubles execution time) catches ordering bugs but not the correctness bugs that are the primary concern.

a2-15 proposes change-guided coverage restricting tests to those referencing modified files. a3-19 identifies that cross-cutting interactions (input->notices->behavior) are untested. Change-guided coverage would further fragment testing by running only tests mapped to changed files, making it even less likely that cross-cutting interaction bugs are detected.

a2-16 provides the diverged=true mechanism for intentional departures from C++. a3-17 notes the C++ reference may encode bugs from version 0.96.4. The tension is that a2-16's mechanism treats divergence as an exception requiring justification, but if many C++ behaviors are actually bugs, the exception mechanism becomes the norm rather than the exception.

a2-06 requires the divergence ledger to flag unreported test cases (tests in the contract but not in the results). a3-04 shows require_golden!() reports tests as 'ok' even when skipped. If skipped tests report as passing, they appear in the results as reported, defeating a2-06's unreported-case detection.

a1-08 insists the stopping criterion must be reliability-based (statistical fault estimation), not coverage-based. a2-09 defines convergence purely in terms of pass/fail/suspicious counts across runs, which is exactly the coverage-based stopping criterion a1-08 rejects as insufficient.

a1-09 warns the frozen oracle permanently bakes in coincident failures. a3-21 formalizes that the frozen oracle converts a relational property to a weaker unary property. Together they compound: not only is the property weaker (a3-21), but the weakness is permanent and cannot be discovered by any amount of Rust-side testing (a1-09).

a1-24 argues golden tests should prioritize code paths most likely to diverge in translation (integer arithmetic, pointer semantics). a2-15's change-guided coverage restricts tests to recently modified files. These prioritization schemes may conflict: a recently modified file may not contain translation-hazardous code, and high-risk code paths may not have been recently modified.

a2-14 detects coincident failures by grouping tests with similar max_diff values within a category. a3-20 warns that shared test infrastructure bugs would cause consistent failures baked into expectations. If the shared infrastructure bug produces consistent behavior across tests, a2-14's coincident failure detection would correctly flag correlation but misattribute the cause to implementation rather than test infrastructure.

a1-12 demands quantitative metrics including distributions of differences. a2-05 reduces these to a binary suspicious/not-suspicious classification using fixed thresholds. The rich quantitative data a1-12 requires is collapsed into a boolean by a2-05's design, losing the distribution information the purist considers essential.

a2-07 enforces that every C++ header has a corresponding Rust file or marker. a3-14 reveals only ~15% of files have golden test coverage. The correspondence auditor ensures structural completeness (files exist) but not behavioral completeness (files are tested), creating a false sense of coverage when structural and behavioral completeness are conflated.

a1-18 requires tight tolerances so only correct implementations pass. a3-24 reveals the uniform 1e-6 layout epsilon represents wildly different relative tolerances (0.1% vs 0.0003%) depending on the scale of values, meaning 'tight' in absolute terms may actually be loose in relative terms for small-valued layouts.

a2-12 requires the self-conformance verifier to run first and gate all subsequent phases. a3-22 identifies translate_cpp_notice_flags as unverified trust with potential symmetric false positives. The self-conformance verifier checks harness structure but not semantic correctness of data translation functions, so it would pass even if translate_cpp_notice_flags has transposition errors.
